Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 1 - The Domestic Violence Act 2018 Architecture
The Domestic Violence Act 2018 is the principal statute governing the civil protective regime for victims of
domestic abuse in Ireland. It also created the coercive control criminal offence under s.39 - the landmark
provision making the patterned, controlling conduct of one partner against another a stand-alone criminal
offence. This chapter sets out the architecture of the Act, the definitions and relationship categories, and the
orders regime, before subsequent chapters take each major element in depth.

1.1 The Architecture - What the 2018 Act Does
The DV Act 2018 consolidates and modernises the protective orders regime previously contained in the
Domestic Violence Act 1996 and creates the s.39 coercive control offence. Its principal architecture:

1.2 The Relationship Categories - Who is Protected
Relevant person definition

1.2.1 Spouses and Civil Partners
Current spouse or civil partner.
Former spouse or civil partner.
Separated spouse (even if not legally divorced).

1.2.2 Cohabitants
Current cohabitant - persons living together as a couple in an intimate and committed relationship.
Former cohabitant.
The 2018 Act removed the prior six-month minimum cohabitation period that had limited the 1996 Act.

1.2.3 Intimate Relationships Without Cohabitation
Dating relationships - the 2018 Act extended protection to persons in intimate relationships who have not lived
together.
This is a significant operational expansion - many high-risk DV cases involve dating relationships rather than
cohabitation.

1.2.4 Parents of a Child in Common
Where two persons are the parents of a child in common, the protective regime applies between them regardless
of whether they were ever in an intimate or cohabiting relationship.

1.2.5 Other Categories
Parent-child relationships in certain circumstances (adult child against parent; parent against adult child).
Persons living together in a non-contractual relationship of a more permanent nature (siblings, certain family
members).

1.4 Breach as a Criminal Offence
Breach of any of the protective orders is a criminal offence under s.33 DV Act 2018. The breach offence is the
principal criminal enforcement mechanism for the protective regime. Chapter 4 treats breach in detail; the
architecture point here is that the civil protective orders carry criminal force - they are not aspirational.
Penalty for breach: 12 months and/or Class B fine on summary conviction (s.33).

1.5 The Coercive Control Offence - s.39 DV Act 2018
S.39 created Ireland's coercive control offence - the criminalisation of the sustained, patterned, controlling
conduct that defines abusive intimate relationships. The offence is treated in depth in Chapter 2; the architecture
point here is the significance:
Before s.39, controlling conduct could only be charged as discrete incidents (assault, threats, harassment). The
pattern was invisible to the criminal law.
S.39 captures the pattern as the offence. The conduct must be persistent; in an intimate relationship; such that
a reasonable person would consider it to have a serious effect on the IP.
Penalty: up to 5 years on indictment - serious offence.

1.6 Procedural Framework
1.6.1 Jurisdiction
Protective orders are made in the District Court. Applications can also be made in the Circuit Court (e.g., as
ancillary in family law proceedings) and in some cases the High Court. The criminal offence under s.39 is
indictable - Circuit Criminal Court trial.

1.6.2 Standard of Proof
Civil protective orders - balance of probabilities.
Criminal offences (s.39, breach of order, DV-context criminal offences) - beyond reasonable doubt.

1.6.3 Anonymity
The 2018 Act provides for the protection of identities in DV proceedings. Reporting restrictions apply in many
circumstances; in camera proceedings are common in family law DV applications.

1.7 Operational Engagement with the Act
Garda engagement with the DV Act 2018 takes two principal forms:

1.7.1 Assisting the IP with Orders
Provide information on available orders.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Refer to court office for application materials; refer to legal aid where appropriate.
Where order is granted, provide copy to all Stations on the IP's beat areas; record on PULSE.
Maintain knowledge of the IP's address and the order terms for enforcement.

1.7.2 Investigating Criminal Offences
Coercive control under s.39 - full criminal investigation framework.
Breach of order under s.33 - criminal investigation; arrest power under s.35.
DV-context criminal offences - assault, threats, harassment, stalking, sexual offences, criminal damage, false
imprisonment - treated in Chapter 5.

1.8 The Child Dimension
Children are present in many DV scenarios. The 2018 Act and the broader child welfare framework apply:
Children First Act 2015 - mandatory reporting obligations for certain professionals; Garda referral procedure
to Tusla.
The 2018 Act extends protective orders to cover dependent children where appropriate.
Children present at DV are themselves potential victims - of direct abuse, of psychological harm, of trauma.
Tusla referral framework operates from the Garda DV scene - the DVLO and Children First procedures.
Children First - Operational Standard
Where a child is present at a DV incident, or where there is reasonable concern about a child's welfare in a DVcontext household, a Children First referral to Tusla is the standard expectation. The Garda Children First Officer
at District level coordinates. Document the referral on PULSE. The referral is not contingent on prosecution
decisions on the criminal side - the welfare obligation is independent.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 2 - Coercive Control - s.39 DV Act 2018
Coercive control under s.39 of the Domestic Violence Act 2018 is the landmark offence created by the 2018
Act. It criminalises the sustained pattern of controlling, threatening, isolating, and intimidating behaviour that
defines abusive intimate relationships. The offence allows the criminal law to capture the pattern itself rather
than only the discrete incidents (assault, threats, criminal damage) that traditionally were the only chargeable
elements. This chapter treats s.39 in depth, with attention to the investigative practice that builds a coercive
control file.

2.1 Definition and Elements - s.39
S.39(1) DV Act 2018

2.1.1 The Elements
Knowingly and persistently: The accused must be aware of their conduct and the pattern must be sustained not a single incident.
Controlling or coercive behaviour: The conduct must be controlling (monitoring, dictating, dominating) or
coercive (compelling through threats, intimidation, pressure). Specific examples in s.39 framework include
restricting freedom, isolating from family/friends, monitoring communications, depriving of necessaries,
threatening, degrading, humiliating, financial control.
Serious effect: Defined in s.39(2) as causing the relevant person (a) to fear that violence will be used against
him or her, OR (b) serious alarm or distress that has a substantial adverse effect on his or her usual day-to-day
activities.
Reasonable person standard: The objective test - would a reasonable person have considered the conduct
likely to have a serious effect.
Relevant person: Spouse / civil partner / cohabitant / former cohabitant / intimate partner (whether or not
cohabiting) - as defined in s.2 DV Act 2018.

2.2 The Statutory Scope of Controlling / Coercive Behaviour
S.39 and the wider framework recognise that controlling and coercive behaviour takes many forms. Common
manifestations the investigation should be alert to:

2.2.1 Isolation
Preventing or restricting contact with family or friends.
Moving the IP away from support networks.
Controlling who the IP can see, talk to, or visit.
Removing or restricting the IP's communications devices.
Disparaging the IP's family / friends to break those ties.

2.2.2 Monitoring and Surveillance
Tracking the IP's movements (phone monitoring, GPS, social media surveillance).
Checking phones, emails, messages.
Demanding accounts of the IP's day, whereabouts, contacts.
Installing tracking software or cameras.
Calling or messaging repeatedly to check on the IP.

2.2.3 Financial Control
Controlling the IP's access to money.
Demanding to see the IP's bank statements / receipts.
Limiting the IP's spending.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Taking the IP's wages.
Building debt in the IP's name.
Restricting the IP's ability to work or earn independently.

2.2.4 Threats and Intimidation
Threats to kill or cause serious harm (overlap with s.5 NFOAP - see Chapter 5).
Threats against the IP's family, children, or pets.
Threats of suicide if the IP leaves.
Threats to take the children, or to reveal information.
Displays of weapons; references to weapons.
Destruction or damage to property as intimidation.

2.2.5 Degradation and Humiliation
Belittling, mocking, name-calling - private or in public.
Sexual degradation.
Forcing the IP to engage in acts the IP finds humiliating.
Public displays of dominance / IP subservience.

2.2.6 Control of Daily Life
Controlling what the IP wears, eats, does.
Setting rules; punishing non-compliance.
Restricting access to food, sleep, medical care.
Restricting the IP's movements (when and where they can go).

2.2.7 Sexual Coercion
Demanding sex; pressuring sexual acts.
Withholding affection / contact as control.
Threats around reproductive choice.
(Sexual offences themselves treated in Volume 6 and Chapter 5 of this volume.)

2.2.8 Technology-Mediated Abuse
Monitoring software on the IP's devices.
Social media impersonation or harassment.
Online disclosure of intimate images (image-based abuse offences).
Use of smart-home devices to monitor / control.

2.3 The Live Incident Response in Coercive Control Cases
Coercive control is rarely 'live' as a discrete incident. The Garda response is typically to a discrete event (an
assault, a threat, a property damage) that is the tip of a coercive control pattern. Live incident response:
Address the immediate offence first - the incident that brought Gardai out.
Separate the parties and create safe space for the IP.
Engage the IP privately - the suspect's presence inhibits disclosure of the broader pattern.
Ask the SCREENING question: 'Is this the first time something like this has happened, or has there been a
pattern?'
Document the IP's disclosure verbatim where possible - the contemporaneous account is the most credible.
BWV captures the IP's demeanour, the scene, the suspect's presentation.
Risk assess - DASH mnemonic and the lethality markers from the front matter.
Children - presence, condition, what they witnessed.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Tusla / Children First referral as appropriate.
Inform the IP of available protective orders.
Refer to local DVLO and victim support services.

2.4 The Investigation Strategy for s.39
A coercive control investigation is a pattern investigation. It draws on multiple evidence streams to build the
picture of sustained, patterned conduct.

2.4.1 The IP Statement - The Foundation
Multi-session statement-taking is often necessary - the pattern unfolds over multiple conversations.
Use of trained DV interviewers / DVLO is recommended.
Statement should establish the relationship timeline, the controlling conduct over time, specific incidents, the
cumulative effect on the IP's life.
Allow the IP control over pace and location of the interview.

2.4.2 Communications Evidence
Text messages - extracted from IP and suspect devices; preserved with metadata.
Voicemails and call records.
Social media - public and private.
Email.
Monitoring software / tracking software (if found on IP devices).
Communications data access where appropriate (statutory framework).

2.4.3 Third-Party Witness Evidence
Family members - have they observed the relationship over time.
Friends - has the IP been progressively cut off.
Colleagues / employer - workplace impact, absences, suspect contact.
Neighbours - sounds, observations, prior incidents.
Healthcare providers - GP, hospital records of injuries and presentations.
Children's teachers / school - if children involved.

2.4.4 Documentary and Financial Evidence
Bank statements showing financial control.
Property records (whose name on tenancy, on bills).
Phone records.
Medical records.
Photographs of injuries over time.

2.4.5 Pattern Documentation
Timeline construction - the relationship, the escalation, the cumulative effect.
Incident log - prior PULSE entries, prior 999 calls, prior protective order applications.
Identification of the 'serious effect' - the IP's life as documented vs. life prior to the relationship / pattern.

2.5 Suspect Strategy and Arrest Power
Specimen Arrest Words - s.39
'[Name], I am arresting you on suspicion of an offence of coercive control contrary to section 39 of the Domestic
Violence Act 2018. You are alleged to have knowingly and persistently engaged in controlling or coercive

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

behaviour against [IP] in such a manner as has had a serious effect on her / him. You are not obliged to say
anything unless you wish to do so but anything you do say will be taken down in writing and may be given in
evidence.'

Power of Arrest
S.39 is an arrestable offence. S.4 CJA 1984 detention regime applies. Where the investigation is significant, the
s.50 CJA 2007 extended detention regime may engage for organised crime contexts (rare in pure DV, possible
where DV intersects with broader gangland activity).

Search Powers
Search of premises requires warrant (Damache); search of devices typically requires warrant. The IP's device
is often crucial - obtain via consent where the IP cooperates, or via warrant where lawfully required. The
suspect's devices are obtained via warrant on premises search.

2.6 Interview Plan - s.39 Suspect
Identification confirmed.
Relationship history - the suspect's account of the relationship over time.
Specific incidents - put each documented incident to the suspect.
Pattern questions - the cumulative behaviour, the suspect's awareness of effect on IP.
Communications - put extracted messages, voicemails to the suspect.
Third-party evidence - put what others observed.
Anticipate defences: relationship dysfunction (not coercive control); IP also abusive (mutual abuse); IP
fabrication.
Inferences from silence - s.18, 19, 19A CJA 1984 may engage; Buck compliance required.

2.7 PULSE / PEMS / File Index - s.39
PULSE incident - DV / coercive control.
Multiple Garda statements (initial responders, DVLO, investigator).
IP statement(s) - multi-session.
Third-party witness statements (family, friends, professionals).
Medical records (with consent / authorisation).
Communications evidence packages (with examiner statement on extraction methodology).
Documentary evidence (financial, property, etc.).
Prior incidents log.
Risk assessment documentation (DV2).
Tusla referrals (where children involved).
BWV from any related incidents.
Custody records, interview transcripts.
Photographs of injuries / scene.
Antecedent statement.
Victim Impact Statement.

2.9 Specimen Statement Wording (Garda Statement Excerpt)
'Following the initial call to [address] on [date], I commenced a coercive control investigation under s.39 DV
Act 2018. I took an initial statement from [IP] at [location] on [date]. Over the following [period], I took a
further [number] statements from her / him supplementing the initial statement and addressing specific
incidents. The pattern she / he described included [briefly: monitoring, isolation, threats, control of finances,
specific incidents on [dates]]. I obtained communications evidence from her / his device with her / his consent
on [date]. I executed a search warrant at [address] on [date] and recovered the suspect's [devices]. Forensic
examination of the recovered devices is set out in the report of [examiner, FSI / Garda Cyber Crime Bureau]. I
interviewed [third-party witnesses]. The cumulative evidence establishes a sustained pattern of controlling and
coercive behaviour over [period].'

2.10 Defence Will Attack
Not a relationship within the statute - the relationship category challenged. Rebut by relationship evidence
(registration of partnership, joint tenancy, shared accommodation, child in common, social media).
Not sustained / persistent - isolated incidents. Rebut by pattern evidence over time.
Not controlling / coercive - relationship dysfunction or mutual conflict. Rebut by specific examples, third-party
observations, IP's documented isolation / restriction over time.
No serious effect on IP - she / he was fine, working, social. Rebut by documented impact (medical, employment,
social withdrawal, mental health).
Not knowing - did not realise the effect. Rebut by IP's repeated communication, third-party observations, the
obvious nature of the conduct.
IP fabrication / motive to lie - vindictiveness, custody dispute, etc. Rebut by corroborating evidence streams
(the case is built on multiple streams precisely for this reason).
Casey / identification - rarely an issue in DV s.39 (identity not in doubt).
Mutual abuse - IP also abusive. Distinguish reactive behaviour by IP from sustained abusive pattern by suspect.

2.11 Red Flag Escalation
Strangulation in any incident - s.4 NFOAP and lethality marker.
Weapons presence / threats - immediate IP safety planning.
Separation / leaving - the highest-risk period; intensify protective measures.
Children at acute risk - Tusla intensive engagement; potential s.13 NFOAP endangerment.
Suspect threats of suicide / homicide-suicide ideation.
Repeat breaches of orders.

2.12 Common Pitfalls

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Treating coercive control as 'just a domestic' or arguing that no specific offence is made out because each
incident in isolation is minor.
Failing to take multi-session statement - the pattern is not captured in a single session.
Failing to engage with third-party evidence - the IP's friends, family, professionals are critical.
Failing to preserve communications evidence early.
Failing to do contemporaneous Tusla referral where children involved.
Failing to risk assess and act on lethality markers.

2.13 Ethics Touchpoints
Standard 1 Duty - the State's positive obligation under Article 2 ECHR (Opuz) where lethality markers are
present.
Standard 4 Use of Force - arrest force minimised; the IP's presence and the children's presence.
Standard 5 Honesty - contemporaneous, verbatim documentation.
Standard 6 Impartiality - same threshold across demographics, relationships, sexualities.
Standard 7 Speaking Up - colleagues minimising DV; raise.

2.14 Compliment Points / Cross-References
S.39 successful prosecutions are formal-commendation worthy - they reflect sustained investigative work over
time.
Cross-reference: Chapter 3 (protective orders accompanying s.39 investigation); Chapter 4 (breach of order
during s.39 investigation); Chapter 5 (DV-context criminal offences within s.39 pattern); Volume 2 (NFOAP
framework underlying); Volume 6 (sexual offences); Volume 8 (sudden death / homicide where DV escalates).

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 3 - The Protective Orders
The Domestic Violence Act 2018 creates a structured regime of protective orders. The orders are civil in nature
- granted by the District Court (and other courts) on the balance of probabilities - but carry criminal force:
breach of any order is an arrestable criminal offence under s.33. This chapter treats each order in detail, with
attention to the Garda role in assisting applicants and in enforcing orders.
Garda role distinction
Gardai do not apply for protective orders on behalf of victims (with limited exceptions). The application is made
by the IP or, in some cases, by HSE / Tusla in respect of dependent persons. The Garda role is to advise IPs of
available orders, to assist with referral to court office and legal aid, to enforce orders once granted, and to take
criminal action on breach.

3.1 Safety Order - s.6 DV Act 2018
Safety Order definition

Statutory Framework
Statute: s.6 Domestic Violence Act 2018.
Court: District Court (Family Law).
Standard of proof: Balance of probabilities.
Duration: Up to 5 years.
Available to: Applicants in defined relationships (spouse, civil partner, cohabitant, former cohabitant, intimate
partner, parents of child in common, certain other relationships).

Order Terms (Standard)
Not to use or threaten to use violence against the applicant / dependent person.
Not to molest or put the applicant / dependent person in fear.
Not to watch or beset the applicant's place of residence.
Where appropriate, additional specific prohibitions (specific persons, locations, communications).

Operational Application
Once an Order is granted, copy is provided by court to Gardai of the area where the IP resides.
Order recorded on PULSE; alert flag against the suspect's record.
Members on beat should be aware of the Order in respect of the IP's address.
Breach of the Order is an offence under s.33 - immediate arrest power under s.35.

Common Issues
Where the parties share accommodation, the Safety Order does not exclude the respondent. Continuing
occupation may itself create tension - Barring Order may be the more appropriate response.
Where the IP is in fear of continued contact, the Order may not by itself be sufficient - safety planning,
relocation, third-party support may be needed.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

3.2 Barring Order - s.7 DV Act 2018
Barring Order definition

Statutory Framework
Statute: s.7 Domestic Violence Act 2018.
Court: District Court (Family Law); also available in Circuit Court.
Standard of proof: Balance of probabilities.
Duration: Up to 3 years.
Available to: Applicants who have proprietary interest in the home OR who have lived in the home with the
respondent for a qualifying period - the 2018 Act eased the historical property qualification.

Order Terms (Standard)
Prohibition on entering the place of residence of the applicant / dependent person.
Not to use / threaten violence.
Not to molest or put in fear.
Not to watch or beset.
May include specific terms - access for collection of belongings (typically supervised by Gardai), contact
arrangements regarding children.

The Property Qualification - Updated by 2018 Act
Previously the 1996 Act limited Barring Orders by reference to property interest. The 2018 Act eased this so
that cohabitants and other relevant persons can apply where they have lived with the respondent in the home
for a qualifying period, regardless of whose name is on the title. This is operationally important - many DV IPs
are in unsuitable housing arrangements; the Barring Order regime now provides protection regardless of legal
title.

Operational Application
Once Order is granted, court provides copy to Gardai.
PULSE alert against the respondent re. the address.
If the respondent is in the home when the Order is granted, the Order takes effect on service - Gardai may be
requested to assist with the respondent's departure (escort to collect essential belongings, etc.).
Breach is an offence under s.33 - arrest power s.35.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

3.3 Interim Barring Order - s.8 DV Act 2018
Interim Barring Order definition

Statutory Framework
Statute: s.8 Domestic Violence Act 2018.
Court: District Court.
Standard: Court must be satisfied that there is an immediate risk of significant harm and protection requires
barring.
Duration: Until the full hearing of the Barring Order application (typically a matter of days / weeks).
On-notice / ex parte: May be made ex parte (without notice to the respondent) in urgent cases; standard
procedure is on notice but with abbreviated timeframe.

Operational Application
Court notifies Gardai immediately on grant.
Service on the respondent is critical - the Order is enforceable from service.
Where Garda assistance with service is requested, follow station procedure.
PULSE alert.
Breach - arrest under s.35.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

3.4 Protection Order - s.10 DV Act 2018
Protection Order definition

Statutory Framework
Statute: s.10 Domestic Violence Act 2018.
Court: District Court.
Duration: Until the full hearing of the Safety Order application.
Standard: Court must be satisfied that the protection of the applicant or dependent person warrants the interim
Order.

Relationship to Other Orders
The Protection Order is the interim version of the Safety Order. It mirrors the Safety Order's prohibitions but is
shorter-term, pending the full hearing. Operationally, the Protection Order and the Safety Order are enforced
in the same way - the operational distinction is duration, not effect.

Operational Application
As for Safety Order - PULSE alert, beat awareness, immediate s.35 arrest power on breach.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

3.5 Emergency Barring Order - s.9 DV Act 2018
Emergency Barring Order definition

Statutory Framework
Statute: s.9 Domestic Violence Act 2018.
Court: District Court.
Duration: Up to 8 working days.
Standard: Immediate risk of significant harm to applicant or dependent person.
Available to: Applicants who do not have a proprietary interest sufficient to ground a standard Barring Order
but who require immediate protection.

The Significance of the Emergency Barring Order
The Emergency Barring Order was a key innovation of the 2018 Act. It addressed a gap in the prior regime:
where the IP was not on the title or did not meet the property qualification of the 1996 Act, the Barring Order
pathway was effectively closed. The Emergency Barring Order provides immediate protection regardless of
title - though it is short-term and intended to bridge to a longer-term remedy (substantive Barring Order,
alternative accommodation).

Operational Application
Often arises in cohabitation contexts where the IP is on lease/title with the suspect but cannot ground a standard
Barring Order.
Immediate service essential - the Order is short-duration.
Garda assistance with service / safe removal of respondent typically required.
Plan for next steps - the IP needs a longer-term protection or arranged alternative.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

3.6 Common Operational Points Across All Orders
3.6.1 The Order Service Process
Court grants Order; copy provided to Gardai of relevant Station(s).
Service on respondent - by Garda, by personal applicant, or by other means as directed by court.
Order takes effect from service in most cases.
Document service - date, time, manner, by whom.

3.6.2 PULSE Recording
Order entered against the respondent's record.
Alert flag specifying terms of Order.
Cross-reference to IP record.
Update on variation, expiration, or revocation.

3.6.3 Operational Awareness
Beat Gardai should be aware of Orders affecting their beat areas.
Briefing material at Station should reflect active Orders.
When responding to incidents at known DV addresses, check PULSE for active Orders en route.

3.6.4 Variation and Discharge
Orders can be varied (terms changed) or discharged (revoked) by the Court on application of either party.
Gardai are notified of variations / discharges - PULSE updated.
Apparent breach should be assessed against the live Order terms - check PULSE for current status.

3.6.5 Reciprocal Orders
Sometimes both parties seek protective orders against each other (cross-applications).
The Court may grant Orders against both parties, against one, or refuse one or both.
Operational vigilance for the underlying dynamic - is one party a primary aggressor with reactive conduct by
the other, or is there mutual aggression.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 4 - Breach of Protective Orders - s.33 DV Act
2018
Breach of a Safety, Barring, Interim Barring, Protection, or Emergency Barring Order is a criminal offence
under s.33 of the Domestic Violence Act 2018. It is the principal criminal enforcement mechanism for the
protective regime - without effective enforcement, the orders are paper. This chapter treats the breach offence
in full operational depth.

4.1 The Offence
S.33 DV Act 2018

Elements
Existence of order: Valid, in force, served on respondent.
Contravention: Conduct that breaches a specific term of the Order.
Identity: The respondent is the person who contravened.
Knowledge / awareness: Implicit - service of the Order on the respondent establishes notice.

Penalty
12 months imprisonment and/or Class B fine on summary conviction (s.33(2)). The offence is summary-only.

4.2 Power of Arrest - s.35
S.35 Arrest Power
This is a robust power of arrest. Reasonable suspicion of breach is sufficient. The member need not have
witnessed the breach personally.

4.3 Live Incident Response
4.3.1 Verifying the Order
Check PULSE for the live Order against the suspect.
Verify the terms in force - terms may have been varied.
Confirm service - the Order is enforceable from service.

4.3.2 Identifying the Breach
What specifically did the respondent do that breaches the Order.
Where (relevant especially for Barring Order - did the respondent enter the prohibited place).
When - specific time.
Who observed - IP, Garda, third parties, CCTV / BWV.

4.3.3 Securing the Scene and the IP
IP safety - the breach itself may indicate escalation.
Risk assessment refresh - re-DASH the situation.
Children if present.

4.3.4 Arrest
Lawful arrest under s.35.
Caution.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Custody processing.

4.4 Suspect Strategy and Arrest Power
Specimen Arrest Words
'[Name], I am arresting you under section 35 of the Domestic Violence Act 2018 for an offence contrary to
section 33 of that Act. I reasonably suspect that you have contravened the [Safety / Barring / Protection / Interim
Barring / Emergency Barring] Order made on [date] by [Court] in respect of [IP]. You are not obliged to say
anything unless you wish to do so but anything you do say will be taken down in writing and may be given in
evidence.'

Notebook / BWV Critical Entries
Order details - type, date, court, terms.
Service details (from PULSE / Order file).
Specific conduct constituting breach.
Time, location, observations.
IP account.
Suspect response on arrest.
Risk assessment.

4.5 Interview Plan
Identification.
Knowledge of the Order - service date, terms understood.
Conduct constituting breach - put the specifics.
Any explanation - mistake, family emergency, IP-initiated contact (does not legalise breach but operationally
relevant).
Awareness of consequences.
Inferences from silence may engage under CJA 1984 framework where applicable.

4.6 PULSE / File Index
PULSE incident - DV / breach of Order.
Copy of Order (from PULSE / court file).
Service record.
Member statement (responding Garda).
IP statement.
BWV.
Any CCTV / third-party evidence.
Communications evidence (where breach is by contact).
Custody record.
Interview transcript.
Charge sheet.

4.8 Specimen Wording - Statement of Member
'At [time] on [date] I and my colleague responded to a call from [IP] at [address]. On arrival [IP] reported that
her former partner [respondent] had attended at the address at [time] and was at the door demanding entry.
PULSE check confirmed an active Barring Order made by [Court] on [date] in respect of [respondent]
prohibiting him from entering or being at the residence. I confirmed with [IP] that the Order had been served
on [respondent] on [date]. I observed [respondent] at the entrance to the property and identified him by reference
to [photograph / IP confirmation / identification]. At [time] I arrested [respondent] under s.35 DV Act 2018 for
an offence under s.33 of the same Act. I administered the caution. [Respondent] replied: "[reply]".'

4.9 Defence Will Attack
Order not in force - expired, varied, discharged. Rebut by current PULSE check and certified court copy.
Order not served - respondent unaware. Rebut by service record.
Specific term not breached - the conduct alleged is not within the Order's terms. Rebut by precise comparison
of Order terms and conduct.
Identification - was it the respondent. Rebut by photo identification, IP confirmation, BWV.
IP invitation / consent - the IP invited the respondent. Distinguish: invitation does not legalise breach where the
Order is in force against the respondent (the Order is between the respondent and the State / court, not
contractual between the parties).
Family emergency / necessity - emergency required attendance. Treat with care; necessity defence is narrow.

4.10 Red Flag Escalation
Pattern of breaches - escalating engagement with s.39 coercive control framework.
Breach with violence - immediate IP safety; s.4 NFOAP if serious.
Breach with weapons - immediate intervention.
Breach with threats to kill - lethality marker; intensive engagement.
Breach at a critical time - separation, court hearing, recent IP disclosure - elevated risk.

4.11 Common Pitfalls
Failing to check PULSE for current Order status before charging.
Assuming IP-initiated contact legalises breach - it does not.
Charging only breach where additional offences also engaged (assault, threats, damage).
Failing to risk-assess the breach in the wider pattern - a breach is rarely an isolated incident.
Failing to refer to Tusla where children involved (breaches at home often expose children).

4.12 Ethics Touchpoints
Standard 1 - the IP is relying on the Garda to enforce the Order. Failure to enforce undermines the protective

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

regime.
Standard 6 - same threshold across cases; no minimisation.
Standard 5 - honest documentation of the breach circumstances; do not embellish nor minimise.

4.13 Compliment Points / Cross-References
Effective enforcement of protective orders is core DV work and compliment-worthy where strong investigation,
robust file, and clear file led to prosecution.
Cross-reference: Chapter 2 (s.39 coercive control - breaches often within a coercive control pattern); Chapter 5
(DV-context criminal offences also engaged); Volume 2 (NFOAP where violence involved).

Chapter 5 - DV-Context Criminal Offences
Domestic violence engages the full criminal-law spectrum. This chapter does NOT re-treat the offences
themselves (treated in Volumes 2, 1, and elsewhere) but addresses how those offences operate in the DV context
- the specific investigative considerations, evidential nuances, and charging dynamics.

5.1 Assault in DV Context
5.1.1 The Offences
The NFOAP offences (Volume 2) apply in their full form:
s.2 NFOAP - assault.
s.3 NFOAP - assault causing harm.
s.4 NFOAP - assault causing serious harm.

5.1.2 DV-Specific Investigative Considerations
Strangulation - the single most predictive lethality marker. Specific protocol: ask the question, document any
answer, medical examination, photograph progression, charge typically s.4.
Pattern of injuries - injuries at different stages of healing indicate sustained pattern.
Location of injuries - typical DV injury patterns include face, neck, arms (defensive), torso (concealed).
Mechanism - manner of infliction may indicate degree of intent.
Defensive injuries - bruising on forearms, scratched hands - support IP's account.
Specific items used - household items as weapons; presence in DV context.

5.1.3 Photograph Protocol in DV Assault
Photograph at scene where possible.
Re-photograph at 24, 48, 72 hours - bruising develops, sometimes appearing only days later.
Photograph with scale (ruler) and IP identifier.
Catalogue and preserve.

5.1.4 The Strangulation Cluster - Operational Specifics
Strangulation in DV - Investigative Protocol
Always ask the question: 'Has there been any choking, strangling, or pressure on your neck?' Document verbatim
response. Recognised symptoms: hoarseness, sore throat, difficulty swallowing, petechiae (small red spots) in
eyes / face, marks on neck (which may appear hours later), loss of consciousness or near-loss, urinary or faecal
incontinence at time, memory loss of the event. Medical examination is appropriate even where no visible injury
- some serious internal damage is invisible externally. Charge: s.4 NFOAP serious harm is frequently the
appropriate charge given the demonstrated risk to life.

5.2 Threats in DV Context
5.2.1 The Offences
s.5 NFOAP - threats to kill or cause serious harm.
s.6 POA - threatening, abusive or insulting behaviour (where in public place; less common in DV context).
Threats can also ground harassment / stalking / coercive control.

5.2.2 DV-Specific Considerations
Threats to kill in intimate-partner context are a lethality marker - charge under s.5 NFOAP where elements are
met.
Conditional threats ('if you leave...') are still threats - the conditionality does not negate the offence.
Threats against third parties (children, family, pets) - the IP's effect is the same; charge as appropriate.
Threats made by communication - text, voicemail, social media - preserve immediately.

5.3 Harassment and Stalking in DV Context
5.3.1 The Offences
s.10 NFOAP - harassment (course of conduct test).
s.39 CJ(MP)A 2023 - stalking (new offence; intense single act or course).
Distinct from s.39 DV Act 2018 coercive control - note the citation overlap; these are different statutes.

5.3.2 Post-Separation Stalking
Post-separation stalking is a high-risk pattern. The recently separated period is the highest-risk period for
intimate-partner homicide. Stalking conduct in this period - tracking, persistent calls, showing up at places,
monitoring through technology - should be treated with the seriousness it merits.

5.4 Sexual Offences in DV Context
Sexual offences within intimate relationships are sexual offences. The marital rape exemption was abolished
by the Criminal Law (Rape) (Amendment) Act 1990 - rape within marriage is rape. The Criminal Law (Sexual
Offences) Act 2017 modernised consent and other sexual offence provisions. Detailed treatment is in Volume
6 (Sexual Offences); this section addresses DV-specific considerations:
The DV context does not reduce the seriousness of the offence - it may aggravate.
Consent must be active, voluntary, and ongoing - the DV relationship context can be relevant to capacity to
consent (coercive control may compromise consent).
Investigation engages specialist support - sexual offence investigation framework, Sexual Assault Treatment
Unit, specialist interviewers.
Charge selection from Volume 6 framework.
Coercive control + sexual offences are cumulative charges.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

5.5 Criminal Damage in DV Context
5.5.1 The Offence
Criminal Damage Act 1991 - principal statute.
Treated in Volume 1; this section addresses DV-specific application.

5.5.2 DV-Specific Considerations
Damage to the IP's property as intimidation - common in DV. Phones smashed (cuts off support); clothing
destroyed (humiliation/control); furniture broken (intimidation by display of force).
Damage to shared property - the legal position is complicated where the respondent has proprietary interest;
but the damage offence can still apply where it is calculated to harm or intimidate the IP.
Damage to pets - in some cases criminal damage / aggravated criminal damage / Animal Health and Welfare
Act 2013.
Pattern of criminal damage over time is highly relevant - both as discrete offence and as part of s.39 pattern.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

5.6 False Imprisonment in DV Context
5.6.1 The Offence
S.15 NFOAP - false imprisonment. Treated in Volume 2; carries life imprisonment maximum sentence.

5.6.2 DV-Specific Application
Detention of the IP in the home - locking doors, taking keys, blocking exits, removing transport.
Restriction of movement over sustained period - s.15 NFOAP may engage as discrete offence alongside s.39
coercive control.
Coercive control often involves restriction of movement - distinguish the pattern (s.39) from discrete detention
episodes (s.15).
Charge stacking: s.15 + s.39 + any specific assault / threats.
S.15 in DV - Often Under-Recognised
S.15 NFOAP false imprisonment carries life imprisonment but is frequently under-recognised in DV
investigations. Where the IP describes being kept in the home, having keys taken, having transport disabled, or
being prevented from leaving - the elements may be made out. Investigate and charge appropriately. The
seriousness of the offence reflects the seriousness of the conduct.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

5.7 Other DV-Context Offences
5.7.1 Image-Based Sexual Abuse
Harassment, Harmful Communications and Related Offences Act 2020 - 'Coco's Law' - criminalises sharing
intimate images without consent and related offences.
Common in post-separation contexts; preserve image evidence under appropriate procedures.

5.7.2 Animal Cruelty
Animal Health and Welfare Act 2013 - cruelty to animals. Pet abuse is recognised as a DV marker and may
indicate escalation.
Cross-reference with criminal damage in some cases.

5.7.3 Endangerment
s.13 NFOAP - endangerment - where conduct creates substantial risk of death or serious harm.
Application in DV: driving recklessly with IP / children; threats with weapons; dangerous use of household
items.

5.7.4 Child-Specific Offences
Children First Act 2015 - mandatory reporting obligations for certain professionals.
Children Act 2001 / Child Care Act 1991 - care framework.
S.246 Children Act 2001 - cruelty to a child.
Children at DV scenes are themselves potentially the victims of offences - investigate as such.

5.7.5 Theft / Financial Abuse
Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001 - theft, deception, fraud.
Financial control in DV may manifest as theft (taking IP's money), deception (compelling IP into transactions
by deceit), fraud.
Where financial abuse is integral to coercive control, consider s.39 DV Act 2018 + applicable theft/fraud
offences.

5.8 Charge Stacking in DV
DV offending frequently involves multiple chargeable offences. A coercive control pattern with discrete
incidents may yield:
Cumulative charging is often appropriate. DPP directions on specific cases; build the file to support each charge
with discrete evidential support while presenting the cumulative picture.

Chapter 6 - DV Homicide and Serious Injury
The high end of the DV escalation curve is homicide. Approximately half of all female homicide victims in
Ireland are killed by an intimate or former intimate partner. The DV homicide is rarely sudden; the precursors
are almost always documented in prior interactions. This chapter addresses the operational and investigative
response to DV homicide and to serious injury cases at the high end of the DV spectrum.

6.1 The DV Homicide Pattern
Research on intimate-partner homicide (IPH) identifies recurring antecedent patterns:
History of non-fatal strangulation - the single strongest predictor.
Recent or impending separation - the highest-risk period.
Threats to kill in the prior period.
Access to firearms / weapons.
Coercive controlling relationship pattern.
Pregnancy or recent birth.
Stalking conduct post-separation.
Suspect mental health / substance misuse.
Prior assaults of escalating severity.
Suspect suicidal ideation (associated with homicide-suicide risk).
The investigation in a DV homicide engages the full Volume 2 (Person Crime) framework alongside the specific
DV dimensions. This chapter focuses on the DV dimensions; refer to Volume 2 for the homicide investigation
framework generally.

6.2 The Immediate Response
6.2.1 Scene Preservation
Standard SIO protocol - Volume 2.
DV scene typically the family home - additional dimensions: children's belongings, family photos, the intimatelife environment.
Document the scene as it speaks to the relationship - signs of conflict, struggle, displays of control.

6.2.2 The Children Present
Children of the deceased and/or the suspect may be present at scene.
Their immediate care - protective response, alternative caregivers identified, Tusla notified.
They may be witnesses - specialist child interviewer; do not interview at scene; SIO and child interview team
coordinate.
Their welfare is a continuing operational concern through the investigation.

6.2.3 The Family
Family Liaison Officer (FLO) assigned per SIO protocol.
In DV homicide, FLO role is particularly sensitive - the family's relationship to the deceased and to the suspect
may be complex.
Family may have warned of the danger previously - the warning record may be operationally relevant and
ethically sensitive.

6.3 The Investigation Framework
6.3.1 The Antecedent Picture
Building the antecedent picture is core in DV homicide investigation. The pattern leading to the killing typically

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

includes:
Prior PULSE incidents - assaults, threats, protective orders, breaches, 999 calls.
Prior protective orders - if any, their currency, breaches.
Communications - messages, calls, social media.
Third-party witnesses - family, friends, neighbours, colleagues, professionals (GP, counsellor, refuge).
Documentary records - medical, financial, employment.
Tusla / social work records (where applicable).

6.3.2 The Forensic Picture
Pathology - cause and mechanism of death; injury patterns; defensive injuries; signs of strangulation;
toxicology.
Forensic biology - DNA, blood, scene biology.
Forensic toxicology - substances involved.
Digital forensics - devices, communications, search history.
Ballistics where firearms.

6.3.3 The Suspect
Identity typically known (intimate / former intimate partner).
Arrest planning - the suspect may be at scene, may have fled, may have attempted / completed suicide.
Custody and interview - standard Volume 2 framework; with DV-specific consideration of relationship
narrative the suspect may present.
Anticipated defences: provocation (especially sustained provocation in DV-context - Heffernan), self-defence
(especially where the killer is the longer-term IP), diminished responsibility (mental health), excessive selfdefence (Dwyer partial).

6.4 The Killer-as-Long-Term-IP Scenario
A specific DV-homicide scenario is the long-term IP who kills the abuser. The literature on 'battered woman
syndrome' and Irish law's evolution on sustained provocation (Heffernan [2017] IESC 5) recognise this
scenario. Investigative considerations:
Document the abuse history thoroughly - the killer's history as an IP of the deceased is operationally critical.
Prior PULSE, medical, social work, refuge records - all relevant.
Family / friend / professional accounts of the abuse pattern.
Mental health history of the killer - effects of sustained abuse.
The immediate circumstances of the killing - what was the precipitating event in context of the longer pattern.
The file should be built to allow the DPP and the trial court to consider the full context. Sustained provocation
(Heffernan) may reduce murder to manslaughter; the file evidence determines whether that path is open. Do
not pre-empt the legal outcome; build the file.

6.5 Homicide-Suicide
A recurring DV-homicide pattern is the suspect killing the IP and then themselves. Investigative considerations:
Confirm the suicide - pathology, scene, communication trail (suicide note, messages).
The investigation continues - establishing what happened, providing the family answers, supporting any
inquest.
No criminal proceedings possible against deceased suspect but the file is the historical record and may inform
civil / inquest proceedings.
Family support sustained - the family has lost the IP and faces complex feelings about the suspect.
Children of the relationship may have lost both parents.
Domestic Homicide Review framework may apply (developing in Ireland).

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

6.6 Strangulation as Attempted Homicide
Non-fatal strangulation in DV is recognised as effectively attempted homicide. The conduct - cutting off the
airway or blood supply, with the potential for death - is grave. Charge selection:
S.4 NFOAP (assault causing serious harm) - frequently the appropriate charge given the demonstrated lethality
risk.
In acute cases approaching the murder threshold, the file may sustain attempted murder.
Pattern of strangulation across the relationship - aggravating context for s.4 charge and for any s.39 coercive
control charge.

6.7 The Pregnancy Dimension
Pregnancy is a high-risk period for DV escalation.
Assaults during pregnancy - injuries to the abdomen, attempts to cause miscarriage - elevate seriousness.
Where death of unborn child results from assault, complex legal questions arise - the Eighth Amendment was
removed but the framework on offences against the unborn remains an area requiring specialist legal advice.
Medical examination of pregnant IP after DV assault - obstetric review, foetal welfare check.

Medical history of IP injuries.
Communications evidence across the relationship.

6.9 The Domestic Homicide Review
Domestic Homicide Reviews (DHRs) are emerging in Ireland following international practice. A DHR is a
multi-agency retrospective review of a DV homicide aimed at identifying lessons and improving response. The
framework is developing; relevant points:
DHR is not a criminal investigation - it is a learning review.
Garda investigation file may inform DHR.
Cooperation with DHR processes supports the systemic improvement of DV response.
Findings can inform Garda training, protocols, and inter-agency working.

6.10 Ethics in DV Homicide
Standard 1 Duty - the State's positive Article 2 ECHR obligation (Opuz line) engages where there were prior
warnings. The investigation should honestly examine whether prior interventions could have been more
effective - this is service to the family and to systemic learning, not blame.
Standard 5 Honesty - the file must reflect what was known and done, accurately.
Standard 7 Speaking Up - where systemic failures contributed, raising via appropriate channels is appropriate.
Family - sustained dignity, sustained engagement, sustained transparency within operational confines.

Appendix - Defences, Burdens, Charge Selection, File
Craft (DV)
This appendix addresses the legal-end practice of DV policing. The burdens of proof framework carries from
Volume 2; this appendix focuses on DV-specific charge selection, the defences likely to be raised in DV cases
(including the developing Irish jurisprudence on sustained provocation), and the file craft for DV criminal
investigations.

Part A - Burdens of Proof in DV
A.1 Civil Burdens (Protective Orders)
For protective orders under the DV Act 2018, the standard is balance of probabilities. The applicant must show
that the order is needed on the balance of probabilities; the court applies the statutory test for each order.

A.2 Criminal Burdens (s.39 Coercive Control, s.33 Breach, NFOAP etc.)
Standard criminal burdens. Prosecution proves every element beyond reasonable doubt. Evidential burden on
defence to put any defence in play; legal burden on prosecution to disprove the defence beyond reasonable
doubt. Insanity / diminished responsibility (rare in DV but possible) - legal burden on defence on balance of
probabilities.

A.3 The Element-Specific Burdens in s.39
S.39 DV Act 2018 has multiple elements; each must be proved by the prosecution:
Knowingly - subjective awareness.
Persistently - sustained pattern, not single incident.
Controlling or coercive - the conduct meets the descriptive standard.
Serious effect on relevant person - the impact element (fear of violence OR substantial impact on day-to-day
activities).
Reasonable person standard - objective check on the conduct.
Relationship - the parties are in a defined category.

Part B - Defences in DV Cases
B.1 The Self-Defence Defence by an IP Who Assaults / Kills the Abuser
A particularly important DV scenario: the long-term IP who eventually assaults or kills the abuser. The
available defences:
Self-Defence (s.18 NFOAP / Dwyer)
Honest belief in need to use force + reasonable force on subjectively believed circumstances.
In sustained DV context, the IP's belief in need to defend may be informed by long pattern of abuse - the
perceived threat from a specific gesture or movement is rooted in history.
Dwyer partial defence (excessive self-defence) may operate in homicide cases - reduces murder to
manslaughter.
Provocation (MacEoin / Heffernan)
Subjective loss of self-control.
Heffernan recognised the possibility of sustained provocation - cumulative pattern triggering loss of self-control
rather than only immediate provocation.
Operationally: build the abuse history thoroughly; document mental health impact; immediate precipitating
event in context.
Diminished Responsibility (s.6 Insanity Act 2006)

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Mental disorder substantially diminishing responsibility.
In DV-context homicide by IP, the cumulative psychological impact of sustained abuse may engage.
Forensic psychiatric evidence essential.

B.2 The Self-Defence / Provocation Defences by Suspect
Conventional defences by the suspect in DV criminal proceedings:
Self-defence claimed against the IP (less common but does arise).
Provocation - the suspect lost self-control - the immediate event context.
Rebuttal: the prosecution case typically shows the suspect as the controlling, escalating party with the IP as the
victim. Build the pattern.

B.3 The Coercive Control Specific Defences
Not a Sustained Pattern
Defence: incidents were isolated, not sustained.
Rebuttal: pattern evidence across time, third-party witnesses, communications, IP statement, prior incidents.
Relationship Dysfunction, Not Coercion
Defence: this was a difficult relationship but not coercive control.
Rebuttal: specific examples of controlling, coercive conduct; the IP's documented isolation, restriction, fear.
Mutual Abuse
Defence: both parties were aggressive; not unilateral abuse.
Rebuttal: distinguish primary aggressor from reactive conduct; the dominant pattern; third-party observations
of the dynamic.
IP Fabrication / Motive to Lie
Defence: IP is motivated to lie (custody dispute, vindictiveness).
Rebuttal: corroborating evidence streams - this is why coercive control investigation builds from multiple
streams (IP, third-party, documents, communications, professional records).

B.4 The 'IP Invited Contact' Defence (s.33 Breach Cases)
Common in breach cases: the suspect argues the IP initiated contact and so the suspect's response was not a
breach. The legal position:
The protective order is between the respondent and the court / State. It is not contractual between the parties.
The IP cannot 'consent' to a breach by the respondent. IP-initiated contact may be operationally relevant (case
management, victim engagement) but does not legalise breach.
The IP's contact may, however, mitigate at sentencing depending on circumstances.

Part E - Trial Practice in DV Cases
E.1 The Recanting / Hostile IP
A common DV trial scenario is the IP who recants or becomes hostile at trial. The prosecution response engages:
s.16 CJA 2006 application - admit the IP's prior statement where IP is intimidated / fearful (Volume 2 Evidence
Appendix).
Treat the IP as a hostile witness if pre-trial application appropriate.
Build the case to be 'evidence-led' rather than 'IP-led' wherever possible - this is why third-party, documentary,
and forensic evidence is built up.

E.2 Pattern Evidence at Trial
S.39 trials by their nature require pattern evidence.
Prior incidents may be admissible as background evidence (Volume 2 Character Evidence treatment).
Multiple discrete charges in one trial may attract issues of admissibility / cross-admissibility.
DPP / counsel direction on trial strategy.

E.3 Expert Evidence in DV
Forensic psychiatry / psychology - particularly in homicide cases (provocation, diminished responsibility,
sustained provocation).
Behavioural / clinical expertise on coercive control dynamics (developing in Ireland).
Medical experts on injury patterns, strangulation, pregnancy-related injuries.

Appendix - Evidence and Admissibility in DV
This appendix addresses the law of evidence as it operates in DV criminal investigations and trials. The general
evidence framework is in Volume 2; this appendix focuses on DV-specific applications. Topics: intimidated /
recanting / hostile IP; recent complaint; pattern evidence at trial; BWV evidence; child evidence; expert
evidence on coercive control.

Part A - The Recanting, Intimidated, or Hostile IP
A.1 The Operational Reality
A significant proportion of DV criminal cases face the challenge of the IP whose account changes between
initial complaint and trial. The reasons vary - intimidation by the suspect, family pressure, reconciliation,
financial / housing dependence, fear of consequences. The criminal-justice response has developed mechanisms
for these scenarios.

A.2 s.16 CJA 2006 - Out-of-Court Statements
S.16 CJA 2006
S.16 is the principal modern statutory inroad on the hearsay rule, particularly relevant in DV:
Application is made to the trial court on notice.
Court considers fairness to the accused; the reliability of the prior statement; the IP's ability to give evidence.
Where granted, the IP's prior statement (typically the contemporaneous Garda statement) is admitted as
evidence.
Strongest cases: where intimidation is documented, where the IP's fear is evidenced, where the prior statement
was contemporaneous and credible.

A.3 Operational Implications for Investigation
Treat every contemporaneous IP statement as if it may be the only IP statement at trial.
Verbatim recording, BWV, audio - the contemporaneity is the credibility.
Document any intimidation, fear, withdrawal as it emerges - the pattern supports the s.16 application.
Anticipate the IP's possible withdrawal - build the case to be 'evidence-led' rather than 'IP-led' - third-party,
documentary, forensic streams.

A.4 The Hostile Witness at Trial
A witness who at trial gives evidence inconsistent with their prior statement, in a way that indicates an
unwillingness to tell the truth (or to support the prosecution case), may be declared hostile by the court. The
prosecution may then:
Cross-examine the witness on the prior statement.
Put the prior statement to the witness.
If adopted, the prior version is part of the evidence.
If denied, the prior statement may be proved by other means (the Garda statement-taker, the recording).

A.5 The Withdrawing IP - the Operational Response
Investigate the reasons - intimidation may itself be an offence.
Engage the IP supportively - the withdrawal may be itself a cry for help.
Refer to victim services - external support may stabilise the IP.
The case may still proceed evidence-led - the IP's withdrawal is not necessarily the end of the prosecution.
Consider witness intimidation as a separate offence (Criminal Justice Act 1999 framework).

Part B - Recent Complaint

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

B.1 The Doctrine
The recent complaint doctrine is most developed in sexual offence cases (Volume 6). In DV more broadly, the
IP's prior complaint may be admissible to rebut suggestions of recent fabrication (Volume 2 Evidence
Appendix) and as part of the narrative.

B.2 In Operation
Where the IP told a third party of the abuse contemporaneously or shortly after, that third party's evidence of
the complaint may be admissible.
Statements to family members, friends, professionals (GP, counsellor) at the time the abuse was occurring are
particularly valuable.
Take statements from these third parties early - they may not be aware of their relevance.

Part C - Pattern Evidence at Trial
C.1 In s.39 Coercive Control Trials
S.39 trials require pattern evidence by the structure of the offence. The prosecution must show the sustained
pattern - this is the case, not background to the case. Pattern evidence is:
Multiple incidents over time.
Multiple categories of controlling conduct.
Multiple sources (IP, third-party, communications, documents).
The cumulative effect on the IP.

C.2 In Single-Incident DV Trials
Where a single discrete incident is charged but evidence of a wider pattern exists, the pattern evidence may be
admissible as:
Background evidence - the context without which the incident cannot be understood (Volume 2 Character
Evidence).
Evidence rebutting any innocent explanation by the suspect.
Evidence supporting the IP's credibility against suggestions of fabrication.
Admissibility is for the trial court to determine on the standard principles - probative value vs prejudicial effect.
Build the file with awareness that pattern evidence may be sought to be adduced.

Part D - Body-Worn Video Evidence in DV
D.1 The Operational Importance
Body-worn video has transformed DV evidence. The BWV from the immediate Garda response captures: the
scene; the IP's demeanour; the IP's verbatim account in the immediate aftermath; the suspect's demeanour; the
children's condition; the injuries; the immediacy that the trial process cannot recapture.

D.2 Activation Protocol
Activate on receipt of DV call where practicable.
Activate prior to entry to scene.
Keep activated throughout incident management.
Brief reassurance to the IP that BWV is active.
Document the BWV activation on the file.

D.3 Evidential Use
BWV is real-time documentary evidence - admitted on conventional basis.
Authenticated by the wearing member's statement.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Preserved with integrity; copies for review; original held by Garda exhibits.
Where IP recants, BWV is independent evidence of the IP's immediate account.
Where suspect contests, BWV captures the contested moment.

D.4 IP Statement on BWV
The IP's account on BWV in the immediate aftermath has high evidential value. It is contemporaneous, captures
demeanour, and resists later recantation. Operational practice: encourage the IP to give a brief account at the
scene where safe to do so (with formal statement to follow at safe location).

Part E - Child Evidence in DV
E.1 The Framework
Children present at DV scenes may be witnesses. The Criminal Evidence Act 1992 provides for video-recorded
interviews of children to be admitted as evidence-in-chief, subject to procedural conditions and the witness
being available for cross-examination.

E.2 Operational Protocol
Children at scene are not interviewed at scene.
Specialist child interviewer (trained in age-appropriate questioning) conducts the interview.
Video-recorded interview at appropriate facility.
Child welfare engagement (Tusla) parallel to evidential process.
Anonymity and identity protections for child witnesses.

E.3 The Child Welfare Priority
Evidence-gathering from children is subordinate to child welfare.
A traumatised child should not be re-traumatised by inappropriate interviewing.
The child's interests are determined within a welfare framework, not solely the criminal-justice framework.
Tusla / Children First leads on welfare; Garda investigation engages within that framework.

Part F - Expert Evidence on Coercive Control
F.1 The Developing Area
Expert evidence on coercive control dynamics, behavioural patterns, and the psychological impact on IPs is a
developing area in Irish DV prosecutions. Where contested behavioural science underlies the prosecution case,
expert evidence may be deployed.

F.2 Common Expert Domains
Forensic psychiatry - mental health, capacity, diminished responsibility.
Forensic psychology - psychological impact of abuse, vulnerability.
Clinical expertise on strangulation injuries (medical).
Clinical expertise on injury patterns over time.
Digital forensic experts on communications evidence.

Part G - Disclosure in DV Cases
G.1 The General Duty
Standard prosecution disclosure duty applies: all material that may assist the defence or undermine the
prosecution case. In DV cases this has particular dimensions:
Tusla records - where engaged, the records relating to the family.

Medical records of the IP.
Counselling / mental health records of the IP (carefully managed; may be subject to privilege).
Refuge / women's aid records.
Prior protective order applications.
Family law proceedings records (may be subject to in camera restrictions).

G.2 Sensitive Disclosure
Some material implicates the IP's mental health, sexual history, or personal life beyond the prosecution case.
Sensitive material disclosure framework operates - DPP / counsel manage.
The IP should be aware that records may form part of the file (consent and information).
Court applications may govern access to sensitive material.

Part H - Witness Protection in DV
H.1 The Spectrum of Protection
DV IPs at risk may need protection on a spectrum:
Practical safety measures - safe house, panic alarm, refuge.
Protective orders enforcement.
Court accompaniment, separate court entrance, screens at court.
Anonymity in proceedings (where statutorily available).
Formal witness protection programme - rare in DV but available in extreme cases.

H.2 The Garda Operational Role
Risk assessment continually.
Safety planning with the IP.
Coordination with refuge / women's aid for safe accommodation.
Multi-agency information sharing within frameworks.
Sustained Garda awareness of the IP's situation through to trial and beyond.

Appendix - Risk Assessment and Multi-Agency Working
in DV
This appendix sets out the operational frameworks for DV risk assessment and for the multi-agency response
that DV cases require. The two are closely linked: risk assessment identifies the level and nature of risk; multiagency working provides the matched response. Both are core operational practice in DV, not optional adjuncts.

Part A - DV Risk Assessment
A.1 Why Risk Assessment Matters
DV risk is not uniform. The risk to the IP - of further violence, of serious injury, of homicide - varies
dramatically across cases. Risk assessment supports a calibrated operational response: at higher risk, more
intensive Garda engagement, more urgent multi-agency referral, more comprehensive safety planning. Risk
assessment also supports the State's positive obligation under Article 2 ECHR (Opuz) - knowing the risk creates
the obligation to respond proportionately.

A.2 The DV2 Risk Assessment
The DV2 Garda risk assessment form is completed at the DV incident scene. Its structure draws on international
DV risk research and identifies the principal lethality markers. Sections include:
Relationship details (parties, history, separation status).
Current incident details.
History of violence.
Strangulation history (specific question).
Threats (kill, harm, self-harm by suspect).
Weapons (access, prior use).
Stalking conduct.
Controlling behaviour.
Recent separation / planned separation.
Children (presence, safety, welfare concerns).
Pregnancy.
Mental health (suspect and IP).
Substance misuse.
Risk rating (typically standard / medium / high).

A.3 The Lethality Markers - Operational Detail
A.3.1 Strangulation
The single most predictive marker. Research indicates non-fatal strangulation increases subsequent intimatepartner homicide risk by 7-8x. Always ask the specific question: 'Has there been any choking, strangling, or
pressure on your neck?' Document the answer verbatim. Symptoms to inquire about: hoarse voice, sore throat,
difficulty swallowing, marks on neck (which may appear hours later), petechiae in eyes / face, loss of
consciousness, urinary or faecal incontinence at time, memory loss. Medical examination is appropriate even
where no visible injury - internal damage may be invisible externally.
A.3.2 Separation
The period of recent separation, planned separation, or attempted separation is the highest-risk period for
intimate-partner homicide. The risk is highest in the first three months post-separation but elevated for up to
two years. Operational vigilance is intensified during separation periods.
A.3.3 Threats to Kill
Threats to kill in DV context are credible. They are particularly dangerous when: explicit, repeated,
accompanied by means (weapons), made shortly before the incident, made post-separation, made in conjunction

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

with strangulation. Charge under s.5 NFOAP where the elements are met.
A.3.4 Weapons
Weapons in the household, or threats involving weapons, are a serious risk marker. Operational response:
seizure where lawfully possible, restraint applications, intensified protective measures.
A.3.5 Controlling Behaviour
Sustained controlling, isolating, monitoring conduct - the substance of the s.39 DV Act 2018 offence - is itself
a risk marker. The IP is less able to seek help, less able to leave, more isolated from supports.
A.3.6 Pregnancy
Pregnancy is a known high-risk period. Assaults targeting the abdomen, attempts to cause miscarriage,
escalating control during pregnancy - all elevate risk. Obstetric medical review of any pregnant DV IP after
assault.
A.3.7 Stalking (Post-Separation)
Stalking is a serious risk marker, particularly post-separation. The conduct of monitoring, following, persistent
unwanted contact in the post-separation period associates with later violence. Charge under s.39 CJ(MP)A 2023
or s.10 NFOAP as appropriate.
A.3.8 Suspect Suicidal Ideation
Suspect threats of suicide if the IP leaves, or generalised suicidal ideation in the suspect, are associated with
homicide-suicide risk. Not a defence to anything; an operational risk factor.
A.3.9 Pattern of Escalation
Increasing frequency, increasing severity, increasing unpredictability of incidents. The escalation pattern is
itself a marker - things are getting worse.

A.4 Acting on the Risk Assessment
Risk assessment is not a paperwork exercise. The risk level identified should drive the operational response:
Risk Level

Typical Response

Standard

Standard DV response - documentation, advice on orders, victim service referral, follow-up

Medium

All standard, plus: DVLO engagement, multi-agency referrals, more proactive follow-up,
consider safety planning

High

All medium, plus: intensive safety planning, MARAC-style multi-agency response, urgent
protective order support, possible specialist DV unit involvement, regular check-ins

A.5 Documentation of Risk Assessment
DV2 form completed at incident.
Risk rating documented on PULSE.
Risk factors specifically noted - not just the headline rating.
Re-assessment at material changes in circumstances.
Risk documentation forms part of the file if criminal proceedings ensue.
The Article 2 Obligation - Operational Reality
The Opuz v Turkey line (2009) and subsequent ECtHR authority establishes State positive obligations to take
operational steps to protect persons known to be at risk of intimate-partner violence. Risk assessment is the
operational mechanism that brings knowledge of risk to the Garda response. Where risk is identified and not
acted on, and serious harm follows, the State response is exposed to Convention review. Risk assessment is not
optional; it is the State's protective mechanism.

Part B - Multi-Agency Working in DV
B.1 The Operational Necessity
DV is not solved by Garda response alone. The IP and the children typically need: housing support, financial
support, legal support, counselling, child welfare engagement, ongoing protective measures. The Garda
response intersects with - and depends on - a wider network of services. Effective DV policing routes the IP
and children to the services they need.

B.2 The Principal Multi-Agency Partners
B.2.1 Tusla (Child and Family Agency)
Statutory child welfare agency.
Children First Act 2015 framework - mandatory reporting, child protection notifications.
Social work intervention for families with children at DV scenes.
Child protection conferences.
Possible care proceedings where children at risk.
Tusla National Office and area-based teams.
B.2.2 Women's Aid / Men's Aid / DV Refuges
National Domestic Violence Helpline (Women's Aid - typically the most-engaged service for female IPs).
Men's Aid - support for male IPs.
Refuges - emergency accommodation for those leaving DV situations.
Outreach and support services - counselling, advocacy, court accompaniment.
Specialist services for migrant women, LGBTI+ persons, persons with disabilities.
B.2.3 Local Authority - Housing
Homelessness response for IPs leaving DV - emergency and longer-term accommodation.
HAP (Housing Assistance Payment) support.
Social housing pathways.
B.2.4 Department of Social Protection
Independent financial support for IPs separating from controlling partners.
Specific DV-related supports.
B.2.5 Health Services
GP - first port for medical evidence and IP support.
Hospital A&E - acute presentation evidence.
Sexual Assault Treatment Unit (SATU) - where sexual offences involved.
Mental health services - for IP, for suspect, for children.
Addiction services - where substance misuse is a factor.
B.2.6 Courts Service
Protective orders pathway - District Court Family Law.
Family law proceedings - separation, custody, access.
Court support services - victim accompaniment.
B.2.7 Legal Aid Board
Free legal advice / representation for those who qualify.
Critical for protective order applications by IPs.
B.2.8 Victim Services Office (Department of Justice)
Victim of crime support framework under the Criminal Justice (Victims of Crime) Act 2017.

B.3 The Garda Coordinating Role

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Gardai are typically the first responders to DV incidents and serve a coordinating role in routing IPs to the
wider network:
DVLO (Domestic Violence Liaison Officer) at District level - the local coordination point.
Children First Officer at District level - the local child welfare coordination point.
National DV / Sexual Crime Unit at Garda Headquarters - national-level coordination, policy, support.

B.4 The MARAC-Style Multi-Agency Risk Meeting
In high-risk DV cases, multi-agency case-conference style meetings (modelled on the UK MARAC - MultiAgency Risk Assessment Conference) bring together: Gardai, Tusla, Women's Aid, health, housing, legal
services. The meeting:
Reviews the case across agencies.
Shares relevant information within information-sharing protocols.
Coordinates response - who does what.
Identifies gaps and accountability.
MARAC-style frameworks are developing in Ireland; the operational principle is the same wherever the model
is applied: high-risk DV needs coordinated multi-agency response, not parallel single-agency responses.

B.5 Information Sharing in Multi-Agency Working
GDPR and data protection framework apply.
Information sharing for safeguarding purposes typically supported by lawful basis (public task, vital interests,
statutory functions).
Children First Act 2015 - specific provisions on information sharing for child welfare.
Operational information sharing typically conducted under inter-agency protocols.
Document the basis and scope of any information sharing.

B.6 The Children First Framework
Where a DV incident involves children present or in the household, Children First obligations apply:
Mandatory reporting persons (Garda included as appropriate) report concerns to Tusla.
Concerns based on the welfare effect of DV exposure on children, not only direct abuse.
Tusla assesses and responds - may engage social work, child protection conference, care proceedings.
Garda Children First Officer coordinates locally.
Documentation - the report, the response, the follow-up.

B.7 The Victim Pathway
A DV IP entering the criminal justice / protective services system typically has multiple touchpoints. The Garda
response should ideally guide rather than leave the IP to navigate alone:

B.8 Common Multi-Agency Pitfalls
Single-agency thinking - the Garda response alone is insufficient; routing to wider supports is core.
Information silos - DV cases need shared awareness.
Inconsistent risk assessment across agencies.
IP fatigue with multiple agencies - coordinated approach reduces burden on IP.
Children invisible - DV response that focuses only on the adult IP fails the children.

Precise statutory wording for the principal DV provisions. Verify on revisedacts.lawreform.ie /
