Part A - Domestic Violence Act 2018 - Architecture
A.1 Section 2 - Interpretation / Relevant Person
'Relevant person', in relation to an applicant under the Act, means a person specified in s.2 - including spouses,
civil partners, cohabitants (current and former, without minimum cohabitation period), intimate partners
(whether or not cohabiting), parents of a child in common, and certain other relationships including parentadult-child.
'Dependent person' - a child of the applicant or respondent (including adopted, foster, step-child) or any other
person resident with the applicant who is reasonably believed to be dependent due to age, infirmity, or disability.

Part B - Protective Orders
B.1 Section 6 - Safety Order
s.6(1): On application to it by an applicant, the court may, if it considers that there are reasonable grounds for
believing that the safety or welfare of the applicant or any dependent person so requires, make an order, to be
known as a 'safety order', prohibiting the respondent from one or more of the following: (a) using or threatening
to use violence against, molesting or putting in fear, the applicant or any dependent person; (b) where the
respondent is not residing at a place where the applicant or any dependent person resides, watching or besetting
any place where they reside.
Duration: Up to 5 years.
Standard: Balance of probabilities; reasonable grounds for believing safety / welfare requires the order.

B.2 Section 7 - Barring Order
s.7(1): On application to it by an applicant, the court may, if it considers that there are reasonable grounds for
believing that the safety or welfare of the applicant or any dependent person so requires, make an order, to be
known as a 'barring order', that the respondent (a) leave the place where the applicant or any dependent person
resides; and (b) until further order of the court or expiration of the barring order, prohibit the respondent from
entering that place; and may also prohibit other conduct as for safety order.
Duration: Up to 3 years.
Standard: Balance of probabilities; reasonable grounds re. safety / welfare.
Applicant qualifying status: Per s.7(2) - relationships and residential history.

B.3 Section 8 - Interim Barring Order
s.8(1): If, between the making of an application for a barring order and its determination, the court is of the
opinion that there is an immediate risk of significant harm to the applicant or any dependent person and the
protection of the applicant or any dependent person requires that an interim barring order be made, the court
may make an interim barring order.
Duration: Until determination of substantive application or 8 working days, whichever is later (per s.8
framework).

B.4 Section 9 - Emergency Barring Order
s.9(1): Where an applicant is not entitled to apply for a barring order because of the property qualification
requirements, the court may, on the application of the applicant, make an order ('emergency barring order') if

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

the court is of the opinion that there are reasonable grounds for believing that there is an immediate risk of
significant harm to the applicant or any dependent person.
Duration: Up to 8 working days.
Ex parte: May be made without notice to respondent in urgent cases.

B.5 Section 10 - Protection Order
s.10(1): If, between the making of an application for a safety order and its determination, the court is of the
opinion that there are reasonable grounds for believing that the safety or welfare of the applicant or any
dependent person so requires, it may make an order ('protection order') containing the prohibitions specified in
s.6.
Duration: Until determination of substantive application.

Part C - The Coercive Control Offence
C.1 Section 39 - Offence of Coercive Control
s.39(1): A person commits an offence where he or she (a) knowingly and persistently engages in behaviour that
(i) is controlling or coercive, (ii) has a serious effect on a relevant person, and (iii) a reasonable person would
consider likely to have a serious effect on a relevant person; and (b) at the time the behaviour occurs, the person
is in a relationship with the relevant person.
s.39(2) - serious effect: Behaviour has a serious effect on a relevant person if the behaviour causes the relevant
person (a) to fear that violence will be used against him or her, or (b) serious alarm or distress that has a
substantial adverse impact on his or her usual day-to-day activities.
s.39(3) - relationship: Refers to the relationships in s.2 of the Act (spouse, civil partner, cohabitant, intimate
partner, etc.).
Penalty (s.39(5)): On summary conviction - 12 months / Class B fine; On indictment - up to 5 years.

Part D - Breach of Orders and Arrest Powers
D.1 Section 33 - Offence of Contravention of Order
s.33(1): A person who contravenes a safety order, a protection order, a barring order, an interim barring order,
or an emergency barring order shall be guilty of an offence.
s.33(2) - penalty: 12 months and/or Class B fine on summary conviction.

D.2 Section 35 - Power of Arrest
s.35: A member of An Garda Siochana may arrest, without warrant, a person whom the member, with
reasonable cause, suspects of an offence under s.33.

DV case law sits at the intersection of Irish criminal jurisprudence (which carries forward from Volume 2) and
a distinctive ECtHR body of authority on the State's positive obligations to protect persons from intimatepartner violence. The Opuz v Turkey line is the operationally significant strand. This appendix treats the
principal authorities in vLex-style depth: Citation / Facts / Holding / Reasoning / Significance / Operational
Application.
Source verification reminder
Citations reflect the position at time of writing. DV jurisprudence continues to develop, particularly the Irish case
law on s.39 DV Act 2018 coercive control prosecutions. Verify on vLex Ireland, BAILII, HUDOC (for ECtHR),
or the Courts Service judgments database. Recent s.39 prosecutions may not yet be reported in citation databases
- check news / criminal courts service listings.

Part A - ECtHR Authority on State Positive Obligations in DV
A.1 Opuz v Turkey (2009) 50 EHRR 28 - LANDMARK
Citation: Application No. 33401/02, judgment 9 June 2009 (Strasbourg).
Facts: Nahide Opuz's mother was killed by Opuz's husband, H.O., following years of escalating domestic
violence against both women. Multiple incidents of violence, threats, and weapon use were reported to Turkish
authorities. Despite repeated complaints and protective measures sought, the husband was not effectively
restrained. He killed Nahide's mother and was eventually convicted of murder. Nahide brought proceedings
against Turkey for failure to protect her and her mother from intimate-partner violence.
Holding: Violations of Article 2 (right to life - in respect of the killing of the mother); Article 3 (prohibition of
inhuman or degrading treatment - in respect of the sustained violence against Nahide); and Article 14
(discrimination read with Articles 2 and 3 - on the basis that the failures reflected systemic gender-based
discrimination in Turkey's response to DV).
Reasoning: The Court established that States have positive obligations under Articles 2 and 3 in respect of
domestic violence. The obligations include: (1) putting in place an effective legislative and administrative
framework; (2) operational obligations to take reasonable steps to prevent harm where the authorities knew or
ought to have known of a real and immediate risk to an identified individual; (3) effective investigation of DV;
(4) effective deterrence and sanction; (5) protection of victims through protective orders and similar
mechanisms. The Court found Turkey had repeatedly failed to take operational steps despite knowledge of risk,
that prosecution responses were inadequate, and that the failures disproportionately affected women - hence the
Article 14 discrimination finding.
Significance: The foundational ECtHR authority on State positive obligations in DV. Establishes that DV is
not a 'private matter' but engages the State's Article 2 / 3 obligations. The 'knew or ought to have known'
standard makes operational risk assessment and response a Convention requirement. The Article 14 dimension
recognises DV as a gendered phenomenon requiring State response.
Operational Application: (1) Risk assessment at the Garda DV scene is the operational mechanism that brings
knowledge of risk to the State response - without it, the 'ought to have known' threshold may be crossed; (2)
Where risk is identified, operational steps must follow - protective orders, monitoring, multi-agency
engagement, intensive Garda response; (3) Investigation of DV must be effective - the standard of Volume 4
file-build; (4) Document everything - the Opuz analysis at trial / inquest level is fact-intensive; the Garda
response file is the State's response record; (5) Where systemic gaps contribute to harm, raising the issue
through appropriate channels supports the State's compliance posture.

A.2 Kontrova v Slovakia (2007) Application No. 7510/04
Citation: Application No. 7510/04, judgment 31 May 2007.
Facts: Mrs Kontrova reported sustained domestic violence including physical abuse and threats by her husband.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

She had attempted to withdraw a criminal complaint under pressure. Police failed to register the complaint
properly and failed to take operational steps. The husband subsequently killed their two children and then
himself.
Holding: Violation of Article 2 in respect of the children. Slovakia failed in its positive obligation to take
operational steps to protect the children where there was a real and immediate risk known to the authorities.
Reasoning: The Court applied the Osman v UK [1998] test for positive Article 2 obligations - whether the
authorities knew or ought to have known of a real and immediate risk to the lives of identified individuals and
failed to take reasonable steps that might have averted the risk. The Court found the police were on notice; the
response was inadequate; the test was met.
Significance: Application of the Osman positive-obligation test to DV. Confirms that the obligation extends to
children in the household.
Operational Application: Children in DV households are within the protective scope. Tusla notification,
multi-agency engagement, and risk-proportionate response apply to the children as well as the adult IP. The
'real and immediate risk' may include risk to children even where the adult IP is the apparent target.

A.3 Bevacqua and S. v Bulgaria (2008) Application No. 71127/01
Citation: Application No. 71127/01, judgment 12 June 2008.
Facts: Mrs Bevacqua and her son S. faced sustained domestic violence and post-separation harassment from
her former husband. Bulgarian authorities failed to provide effective protection through criminal investigation
or civil protective measures.
Holding: Violation of Article 8 (right to private and family life). The State failed in its positive obligation to
provide effective protection to a DV victim and her child.
Reasoning: The Court extended the Article 8 framework (private and family life) as encompassing protection
from intimate-partner abuse. Failure to provide effective civil and criminal remedies for DV breached the
positive Article 8 obligation.
Significance: Broadens the Convention basis for DV positive obligations - not only Article 2/3 but Article 8.
The effective-remedy dimension extends to civil protective measures and not solely criminal prosecution.
Operational Application: The Garda role in supporting protective orders, in advising IPs of available
remedies, and in enforcing orders is part of the Article 8 protective framework. Failure to enforce orders
effectively engages the Convention dimension.

A.4 Branko Tomasic and Others v Croatia (2009) Application No. 46598/06
Citation: Application No. 46598/06, judgment 15 January 2009.
Facts: The applicant's daughter and granddaughter were killed by the daughter's partner, who had been
convicted of threats to kill them and had been ordered to undergo psychiatric treatment - but the treatment had
not been provided. He killed them and himself shortly after release.
Holding: Violation of Article 2. The authorities had specific knowledge of the threat and had failed to take the
steps that the court orders required (provision of psychiatric treatment), with foreseeable lethal consequences.
Reasoning: The Court applied the Osman test. The threat was specific and known; the State response was
inadequate (failure of statutory psychiatric treatment); the failure was causally connected to the deaths.
Significance: Extends the positive obligation to require effective implementation of protective measures - not
merely their order. The State must follow through on protection.
Operational Application: Protective orders are not paper. The Garda enforcement, the multi-agency
execution, the practical follow-through - all are part of the State's compliance with the positive obligation.
Where the system fails the IP after orders are made, the Convention dimension engages.

A.5 Talpis v Italy (2017) Application No. 41237/14
Citation: Application No. 41237/14, judgment 2 March 2017.
Facts: Mrs Talpis reported sustained DV by her husband to Italian authorities. Despite reports, the police
response was slow and inadequate. The husband eventually attacked Mrs Talpis, killing their son who attempted
to protect her, and seriously injuring her.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Holding: Violation of Article 2 (death of son), Article 3 (violence against Mrs Talpis), and Article 14 read with
Articles 2 and 3 (gender-based discrimination in State response).
Reasoning: The Court reiterated the Opuz framework. Italy had specific knowledge of escalating risk and had
failed to take reasonable operational steps. The systemic delay and inadequacy reflected gendered patterns in
the State's DV response.
Significance: Reaffirms and applies the Opuz framework. Indicates the standard is being applied robustly. The
Article 14 dimension is sustained.
Operational Application: Same as Opuz - knowledge of risk plus failure to take operational steps engages the
Convention. The reasonableness of the steps taken is the operational question - delay and minimisation are the
pattern of failure.

A.6 A v Croatia (2010) Application No. 55164/08
Citation: Application No. 55164/08, judgment 14 October 2010.
Facts: The applicant 'A' faced sustained DV from her husband over several years, including threats with
weapons. Multiple criminal proceedings were initiated against the husband but none reached effective
conclusion. Civil protective orders were not effectively enforced.
Holding: Violation of Article 8. The State failed in its positive obligation to provide effective protection.
Reasoning: The Court found systemic inadequacy in the Croatian response - prolonged proceedings,
ineffective enforcement, and lack of coordination across the protective framework. The cumulative inadequacy
violated Article 8.
Significance: Reinforces that the positive obligation is one of effective protection - the procedural mechanisms
exist but must work in practice. Cumulative inadequacy across separate proceedings is itself a Convention issue.
Operational Application: Sustained Garda engagement across the duration of the protective framework is
operationally important. The IP should not have to start from scratch with each new incident or each new
agency. Continuity, file integration, and multi-agency coordination support effective protection.

A.7 Osman v UK (1998) 29 EHRR 245 - The Foundational Positive Obligation Test
Citation: Application No. 23452/94, judgment 28 October 1998.
Facts: Not a DV case directly - concerned a teacher who developed an obsession with a pupil, eventually killing
the pupil's father and injuring the pupil. The applicants sought to hold the State liable under Article 2 for failure
to prevent the killings despite multiple prior warnings.
Holding: The State's positive obligation under Article 2 arises where the authorities knew or ought to have
known of a real and immediate risk to the life of an identified individual from the criminal acts of a third party,
and where they failed to take measures within the scope of their powers which, judged reasonably, might have
been expected to avoid that risk.
Reasoning: The Court framed the positive obligation test in carefully bounded terms - actual or constructive
knowledge; real and immediate risk; identified individual; reasonable measures available. The test imposes a
real obligation but does not require the State to prevent all foreseeable harm.
Significance: The foundational test that the DV-specific positive-obligation cases (Opuz, Kontrova, Branko
Tomasic, Talpis) apply. The 'Osman test' is the analytical framework.
Operational Application: The Garda response to DV is assessed against the Osman test where serious harm
follows. The factors that engage the State response:
Actual knowledge - Garda reports, prior incidents, current presentation.
Constructive knowledge - what should have been understood from the available information.
Real and immediate risk - lethality markers, escalation, threat specifics.
Identified individual - the IP, the children, identified third parties.
Reasonable measures - protective orders, intensified monitoring, multi-agency engagement, IP safety planning,
criminal sanction.

Part B - Irish Authority Carrying to DV Context
B.1 The People (DPP) v MacEoin [1978] IR 27 - Provocation
Citation: [1978] IR 27 (Court of Criminal Appeal).
Facts: MacEoin was convicted of murder. He claimed provocation - sustained verbal and physical aggression
by the deceased culminating in the killing. The CCA considered the operative test for provocation in Irish law.
Holding: Provocation in Irish criminal law is subjective. The accused must have actually lost self-control as a
result of the provocation. The reasonable-person standard from English law (Camplin) is rejected as
inconsistent with the subjective nature of the inquiry.
Reasoning: Kenny J framed the test as a personal inquiry into the accused's state of mind. The accused's
individual characteristics, sensitivities, and reactions are relevant - not the hypothetical reasonable person.
Significance: Foundational Irish authority on subjective provocation. Distinguishes Irish law from the English
Camplin position.
Operational Application (DV): In DV-homicide cases where the killer is the long-term IP claiming
provocation, the subjective test means the accused's specific psychological state - shaped by the abuse history
- is relevant. Build the abuse history; build the immediate context; the law allows the subjective analysis.

B.2 The People (DPP) v Davis [2001] 1 IR 146 - Provocation Threshold
Citation: [2001] 1 IR 146 (Court of Criminal Appeal).
Significance: Reinforces and refines MacEoin. Mere anger or upset is insufficient - the loss of self-control
must be sudden and temporary, and must arise from the provocation.
Operational Application: In DV-homicide provocation cases, the immediacy of the loss of self-control
matters. Sustained simmering anger without a specific triggering event is harder to fit into the Davis framework.

B.3 The People (DPP) v Heffernan [2017] IESC 5 - Sustained Provocation
Citation: [2017] IESC 5 (Supreme Court).
Facts: Heffernan killed his wife in circumstances that involved a sustained pattern of conflict, mental health
issues, and an immediate precipitating event. The defence raised provocation including the cumulative impact
of the relationship dynamics.
Holding: Provocation in Irish law accommodates cumulative or sustained provocation, not only immediate
provocation. The killer's state of mind at the time of the killing is the inquiry; the contribution of sustained prior
provocation is relevant.
Reasoning: The Supreme Court extended the MacEoin / Davis subjective framework to accommodate
cumulative provocation. The immediate event may be the trigger; the accumulated abuse / pressure is part of
the subjective state-of-mind picture.
Significance: Important development for the DV-homicide scenario where the killer is the long-term IP.
Heffernan opens the legal pathway for the sustained-abuse narrative to operate as partial defence.
Operational Application (DV): In DV-homicide cases where the killer is the long-term IP, build the abuse
history thoroughly. Heffernan means the court will hear cumulative provocation. The investigation file should
capture the full pattern - prior PULSE, medical, social work, refuge, third-party witnesses. Do not pre-empt the
legal outcome; build the file to allow informed decision-making.

B.4 The People (DPP) v Dwyer [1972] IR 416 - Excessive Self-Defence
Citation: [1972] IR 416 (Supreme Court).
Holding: Where the first limb of self-defence is met (honest belief in need to use force) but the second is not
(the force used was excessive), the partial defence reduces murder to manslaughter. The accused is not
convicted of murder but of manslaughter.
Significance: The 'Dwyer partial defence' - unique to Irish law. Operates where the accused had genuine selfdefence belief but used disproportionate force.
Operational Application (DV): In DV-homicide cases where the killer (whether long-term IP or otherwise)

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

used force claimed to be self-defensive but disproportionate, Dwyer may operate. The investigation should
document the proportionality picture - force used, opportunity to disengage, weapons, sequence.

B.5 The People (DPP) v Murray [1977] IR 360 - Subjective Recklessness
Citation: [1977] IR 360 (Supreme Court).
Significance: Foundational subjective recklessness (treated in full in Volumes 2 and 3).
Operational Application (DV): Applies to recklessness limbs across DV offending - threats (s.5 NFOAP
recklessness), endangerment (s.13 NFOAP), and the 'reckless' analysis in s.39 DV Act 2018 to the extent the
prosecution must establish the suspect's awareness of effect.

B.6 The People (DPP) v Walsh [1980] IR 294 - Arrest Reasonable Suspicion
Citation: [1980] IR 294 (Supreme Court).
Significance: Foundational arrest threshold (treated in full in Volumes 2 and 3).
Operational Application (DV): Applies to every DV arrest. Notebook must capture the arresting member's
reasonable suspicion. For s.35 DV Act 2018 arrests (breach), the suspicion is of contravention of a specific
Order term - the notebook should specify.

B.7 The People (DPP) v Madden [1977] IR 336 - Voluntariness of Statement
Citation: [1977] IR 336 (Supreme Court).
Significance: Foundational voluntariness for admissibility (treated in Volume 2).
Operational Application (DV): In DV interviews - both of suspects and of IPs - voluntariness governs
admissibility. The IP statement taken under pressure, in fear, or in the suspect's presence may be challenged on
voluntariness grounds (rare; IP statements are not 'against' the IP but their reliability and admissibility can be
tested).

B.8 DPP v Gormley and White [2014] IESC 17 - Pre-Interview Solicitor
Operational Application (DV): Carries fully. DV suspects in custody have the pre-interview consultation
right. Particular sensitivity given DV context - the suspect may already have strategic awareness about IP
statement vulnerability; legal advice is the suspect's right.

B.9 DPP v Doyle [2017] IESC 1 - Solicitor in Interview
Operational Application (DV): Carries fully. Where suspect requests solicitor presence in interview, facilitate.

B.10 DPP v Buck [2002] 2 IR 268 - Inference Prerequisites
Operational Application (DV): Where s.18 / 19 / 19A CJA 1984 inferences are sought against a DV suspect,
strict Buck compliance. The DV context does not relax the prerequisites.

B.11 People (AG) v Casey (No.2) [1963] IR 33 - Identification Warning
Operational Application (DV): Identification is typically not in issue in DV cases (the parties are known to
each other). Where identification is in issue (e.g., post-separation incidents where the suspect's identity is
disputed), the Casey framework applies.

B.12 DPP v JC [2015] IESC 31 - Modern Exclusionary Rule
Operational Application (DV): Carries fully. Searches of suspect premises / devices, communications data
access, and other intrusive measures must comply rigorously with the legal framework. The JC margin for
inadvertent breach is narrow.

B.13 Damache v DPP [2012] IESC 11 - Warrant Independence
Operational Application (DV): Search warrants for DV suspect premises / devices to court / independent
authority. In urgent post-incident scenarios where the suspect has just left scene, the standard arrest / detention

framework operates; subsequent premises searches require warrants.

Part C - Developing Irish Authority on s.39 DV Act 2018
The Irish case law on s.39 coercive control is developing rapidly. Since the offence took effect on 1 January
2019, prosecutions have proceeded and case law is emerging - though specific reported appellate authority
remains limited. Operational practitioners should:
Monitor the Court of Appeal judgments database for s.39 appellate decisions.
Monitor Circuit Criminal Court reporting (typically via media) for s.39 first-instance outcomes.
Be alert to emerging guidance on: the 'persistent' threshold; the 'serious effect' threshold; the use of pattern
evidence; expert evidence on coercive control dynamics; sentencing patterns.
Engage with the National Garda DV/Sexual Crime Unit for updates on prosecution-side learning.
Consult vLex Ireland and the Bar of Ireland practice publications for evolving commentary.
Key questions on which Irish authority is developing:
How is 'persistent' assessed - over what period and frequency.
How is 'serious effect' established at trial - reliance on IP account, third-party observation, expert evidence.
How is pattern evidence presented and how does it interact with discrete incident evidence.
How are charges typically stacked between s.39 and discrete NFOAP / criminal damage / breach charges.
Sentencing patterns - the 5-year indictable maximum and how courts position cases within the available range.

Part D - Sentencing Authority in DV
D.1 The People (DPP) v Tiernan [1988] IR 250 - Structured Discretion
Operational Application (DV): Sentencing in DV follows Tiernan structured discretion. Aggravating factors
specific to DV:
Victim vulnerability - emotional, financial, immigration dependence.
Children present - exposed to or affected by the offending.
Strangulation involvement - serious aggravator.
Sustained pattern over time.
Breach of trust within intimate relationship.
Use of weapons.
Pregnancy victim.
Post-separation context.
Prior orders / prior breaches.
Conduct designed to terrorise or to demonstrate power.

D.2 The People (DPP) v M [1994] 3 IR 306
Operational Application (DV): Refines the Tiernan framework. Carries to DV sentencing analysis.

D.3 Sentencing Patterns in DV (General Observations)
S.39 coercive control - sentencing patterns developing; ranges include substantial custodial sentences in cases
involving sustained serious pattern and significant impact on IP.
S.4 NFOAP serious harm in DV strangulation - substantial custodial sentences, recognising lethality risk.
S.5 NFOAP threats to kill in DV - significant custodial sentences where threats are credible and accompanied
by means.
S.33 DV Act 2018 breaches - first-breach often summary disposal with appropriate sanction; repeated breaches
attract progressively serious sentencing.
DV homicide - typical murder sentences (life); manslaughter (where provocation, sustained provocation, or
excessive self-defence operates) - wide range depending on case-specific factors.

Part E - Key Operational Takeaways from Case Law
E.1 The Opuz Operational Imperative
The State's Article 2/3 positive obligations under Opuz translate operationally to: (a) effective risk assessment
at every DV scene; (b) proportionate operational response to identified risk; (c) effective investigation and
prosecution; (d) effective enforcement of protective measures; (e) multi-agency engagement where the risk
requires it. The Garda DV file is the State's response record.

E.2 The Sustained Provocation Pathway (Heffernan)
In DV homicide where the killer is the long-term IP, Heffernan opens the legal pathway for cumulative
provocation as partial defence. The investigation file must build the abuse history thoroughly.

E.3 Strangulation as Lethality Marker (Operational, not Strictly Legal)
The lethality research on strangulation is now embedded in operational practice and supported by sentencing
aggravation. Charge typically s.4 NFOAP; document specifically; medical examination.

E.4 The Civil-Criminal Interface
DV operates across civil (protective orders) and criminal (s.33 breaches, s.39 coercive control, NFOAP, etc.)
frameworks. Bevacqua and A v Croatia confirm that effective protection requires both, and that failure on either
side engages Convention obligations.

E.5 Children in DV
Kontrova confirms that children in DV households are within the protective scope of the State's obligations.
The Children First framework provides the operational mechanism.

E.6 The Procedural Compliance Framework
The Volume 2 procedural framework (Madden, Gormley, Doyle, Buck, Casey, JC, Damache) applies to DV
investigations. The DV context does not relax procedural requirements; it intensifies the need for compliance
because DV cases are often contested with high-stakes consequences.

How to Use Volume 5
Volume 5 covers drugs policing - one of the highest-volume operational areas across DMR North Central. The
Misuse of Drugs Act 1977 (as amended) is the principal statute; the volume also covers the Criminal Justice
(Psychoactive Substances) Act 2010, the Criminal Justice (Drug Trafficking) Act 1996, and the interface with
proceeds-of-crime legislation. The structure follows the established Volumes 1-4 pattern:

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

The Drugs Operational Framework
Drug policing operates across a spectrum from individual user possession to organised criminal supply. The
same statute (MDA 1977) carries offences from Class E fine summary disposition through 10-year-and-above
indictable sentences. The investigative response is calibrated by:

The Type of Drug
Class A - heroin, cocaine, MDMA / ecstasy, methamphetamine, LSD - highest concern.
Class B - amphetamines (some), cannabis resin / herbal cannabis in certain contexts.
Class C - benzodiazepines, anabolic steroids, certain other controlled drugs.
Psychoactive substances - the CJ(PS)A 2010 framework for substances not specifically scheduled.

The Quantity
Personal-use amounts - typically s.3 possession.
Larger quantities - presumption of supply (s.15).
EUR 13,000+ value - s.15A presumptive minimum 10-year sentence regime engages.
Major importation / wholesale quantities - serious organised crime framework.

The Role of the Suspect
User - personal use, often with addiction context; vulnerability and welfare engagement.
Street dealer - supply to users; range of involvement and organisation.
Mid-level supplier - bulk handling, supply chain role.
Importer / wholesaler - high-end organised crime.
Facilitator - drivers, premises holders, money handlers.

The Operational Context
Stop-and-search arising from observation.
Targeted operation following intelligence.
Search warrant execution at premises.
Vehicle stop with drugs found.
Custody discovery (drugs found on suspect arrested for other offence).

Garda Code of Ethics - Drugs Application
Drugs policing engages distinctive ethics considerations. The user-dealer spectrum spans persons in addiction
crisis to organised criminal actors. The use of intrusive search powers requires restraint and proportionality.
The public health interface means the Garda response is not solely a law-enforcement function. Five Standards
apply with particular salience.

Standard 1 - Duty
Drug-related harm is real and serious - to users, families, and communities. The Garda duty to protect life and
the public includes effective drug enforcement at the supply end. It also includes recognising that prosecution
of vulnerable users is not always the right response - diversion to health services, the cannabis 'Cautioning'
Scheme (where applicable), and engagement with addiction support may serve the protective function better.

Standard 4 - Use of Force
Force in drug enforcement is sometimes necessary - in the execution of warrants on premises, in the arrest of
dealers, in dealing with intoxicated and unpredictable persons. Force must be lawful, necessary, proportionate.
The Garda Public Order Unit and specialised tactical resources support high-risk operations.

Standard 5 - Honesty and Integrity
Drugs evidence is technically demanding and integrity-sensitive. Chain of custody, exhibit handling, statement
accuracy, FSI sample handling - all demand exacting honesty. The temptation to cut corners on technical
procedure is a serious ethics risk - drugs cases are routinely contested on procedural grounds and shortfalls
undermine prosecution and the wider integrity of Garda investigation.

Standard 6 - Impartiality
Drug enforcement engages communities differentially. The Garda response must be impartial - same threshold
of intervention across demographics, neighbourhoods, communities. Targeting based on appearance, ethnicity,
or socioeconomic indicators rather than specific evidence is a Standard 6 trip-wire.

Standard 7 - Speaking Up
Drugs investigations sometimes involve covert techniques, informants, or operations where procedural
shortcuts may be tempting. The duty to speak up against irregularities - in evidence handling, in source
management, in investigative integrity - is operationally critical. Internal channels (Sergeant, Inspector, Garda
Drugs Unit supervision, Garda Síochána Ombudsman Commission) are available.

The Five Drug-Investigation Ethics Trip-Wires

Compliments - Drug-Investigation Categories
Strong stop-and-search judgment - well-grounded cause, professional conduct, clean evidential outcome.
Complex investigation work - sustained surveillance, intelligence-led operations, financial profiling.
Compassionate engagement with vulnerable users - the difficult judgment that diversion was the right call.
Major seizure / operation - formal commendation level work.
Multi-agency coordination - CAB referrals, Customs joint operations, international cooperation.

The Public Health Interface
Drug use sits at the intersection of criminal justice and public health. Operational realities:
Many users are persons in chronic addiction with associated mental health and welfare needs.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

HSE addiction services, methadone programmes, harm reduction services are part of the wider response.
The cannabis 'Cautioning' Scheme (where in operation) provides diversion for first-offence simple cannabis
possession - check current policy.
Naloxone availability and use by Gardai in opioid overdose response is part of operational practice.
Drug-related deaths investigation engages both criminal (Vol 8 sudden death framework) and public health
learning frameworks.

Specific Vulnerability - The Detained Person Under Influence
A person detained on drug-related grounds may themselves be intoxicated, in withdrawal, or in addiction crisis.
Custody-stage care:
Medical assessment at custody where intoxication / withdrawal evident.
Interview only when fit (voluntariness implications under Madden).
Mental health considerations where dual diagnosis.
Naloxone available in opioid overdose risk.
Healthcare referrals on release where appropriate.
